answer:
**Charles Comte** provides the most explicit and systematic treatment of the relationship between morals (*moeurs*, or habits) and laws (*lois*) in his *Traité de Législation*, Vol. I. He treats both as parallel domains within the "moral sciences," studied via an analytical method that describes their causes (in men or things), nature, and effects (good or bad) on human prosperity, without prescribing duties or relying on religion or sentiment.[^1] Morals concern habits (e.g., intemperance vs. economy), while laws are powers composed of force elements affecting society; yet both improve through enlightenment and observation of utility: as peoples advance in knowledge, vices decline, virtues rise, and legislation perfects (e.g., abolition of torture, freer commerce).[^2] They are interdependent—vicious habits produce bad laws, good habits enable better ones—but analytically independent: neither is derived from the other, and both trace to primitive facts (organization, climate, etc.). Religion cannot be their exclusive foundation, as this halts progress by excluding fact-based inquiry.[^3]

**Charles Dunoyer** does not offer a comparably direct or "strong" formulation in the available works (*Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vols. I-II). He invokes "morality" (*morale*) repeatedly in discussing industrial liberty (e.g., as "good personal morality" and "good relational morality" enabling free industry), but pairs it more with habits/culture than laws explicitly.[^4] No chapter wiki or search yields a focused analysis akin to Comte's; instead, morality appears as a power of labor/faculties supporting prosperity under free regimes, alongside knowledge and capital.[^5]

Comte's account thus allows **more independence** between morals and laws (parallel empirical sciences, neither foundational to the other). Dunoyer's (inferred) view implies greater **moral priority** to viable laws/liberty, hence more vulnerable to the reverse objection: that laws/regulations shape morals (e.g., oppressive regimes corrupt habits, as Comte details).[^6]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch.2, ¶1](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/#p-5219cf).
[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch.2, ¶24-27](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/#p-5f9167).
[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch.8](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/08-du-systeme-qui-fait-d-une-religion-positive-le-fon/translation/#p-89a0f7).
[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [ch.15, ¶?](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/15-v-application-de-ces-moyens-de-liberte-aux-diverse/translation/#p-6d9be7).
[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [ch.14](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-85d580).
[^6]: Corpus searches ("morals" "laws"; "morality" "law(s)"; "morale" "lois"/"législation"; variants) returned no concentrated Dunoyer passages; his emphasis on morality enabling "liberté compatible" across regimes suggests precedence over formal laws.[^?]
